subtopic_detail: Most effective EC at 96 hours UPSI
stem: A 22-year-old nulliparous woman presents to her GP requesting emergency contraception. She had a single act of unprotected vaginal intercourse 96 hours ago. Her last menstrual period began 12 days ago and her cycles are regular at 28 days. She has no contraindications to hormonal or non-hormonal contraception and wishes to also commence ongoing contraception. Her BMI is 26.

data_table.Examination: Vital signs within normal limits; abdomen soft and non-tender; no current vaginal bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative; NAAT for chlamydia and gonorrhoea sent

lead_in: Which is the single most effective emergency contraception option?

options.text: Insertion of a copper intrauterine device within the next 24 hours
options.rationale: The copper IUD is effective up to 5 days after unprotected intercourse and is the most effective emergency contraception with a failure rate under 0.1%; it also provides ongoing long-acting reversible contraception. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Emergency contraception)
source_refs: Family Planning Australia: Emergency contraception

options.text: Oral levonorgestrel 1.5 mg as a single dose taken as soon as possible today
options.rationale: Oral levonorgestrel is licensed within 72 hours of intercourse and efficacy falls off thereafter; at 96 hours it is outside the recommended window. (Source: eTG Sexual health: Emergency contraception)
source_refs: eTG Sexual health: Emergency contraception

options.text: Oral ulipristal acetate 30 mg as a single dose taken as soon as possible today
options.rationale: Ulipristal acetate is effective up to 120 hours but is less effective than the copper IUD and does not provide ongoing contraception. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Emergency contraception)
source_refs: Family Planning Australia: Emergency contraception

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive Yuzpe regimen with high-dose oestrogen and progestogen
options.rationale: The Yuzpe regimen is now obsolete in Australian practice and has higher failure rates and more nausea than levonorgestrel, ulipristal or the copper IUD. (Source: eTG Sexual health: Emergency contraception)
source_refs: eTG Sexual health: Emergency contraception

options.text: Reassurance as conception is unlikely beyond the standard emergency contraception window
options.rationale: Conception remains possible after a single act of unprotected intercourse, and the copper IUD remains effective up to 5 days; reassurance alone is inappropriate. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Emergency contraception)
source_refs: Family Planning Australia: Emergency contraception

explanation.summary: The copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraception with a failure rate under 0.1% and remains effective up to 5 days after unprotected intercourse, with the added benefit of ongoing long-acting reversible contraception. Levonorgestrel efficacy falls beyond 72 hours; ulipristal extends to 120 hours but is less effective than the copper IUD.
key_points: Copper IUD is the most effective EC, working up to 5 days after UPSI
Levonorgestrel is recommended within 72 hours; ulipristal up to 120 hours
Yuzpe combined oral contraceptive regimen is now obsolete
explanation.pearls: Insert the copper IUD at the same visit when feasible; if delayed, use ulipristal acetate as a bridge and recheck pregnancy testing before later IUD insertion.

stem: A 30-year-old G1 attends her first antenatal appointment at 12 weeks. Routine booking bloods return HBsAg positive, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe positive, ALT 38, HBV DNA 5,000 IU/mL. She is otherwise well, has no symptoms of liver disease, and her physical examination is unremarkable. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. She plans a vaginal birth at term in a tertiary maternity unit.

data_table.Examination: No stigmata of chronic liver disease, no hepatosplenomegaly, mucous membranes pink
data_table.Investigations: HBsAg positive, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe positive, anti-HBc IgG positive; ALT 38 (normal less than 35), AST 30; HBV DNA 5,000 IU/mL; HIV and hepatitis C negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate plan for the neonate at birth?

options.text: Routine immunisation schedule commencing with hepatitis B vaccine at 6 weeks of age only
options.rationale: Routine 6-week vaccination alone is inadequate for an infant of an HBsAg positive mother; birth-dose vaccine plus HBIG is required to prevent vertical transmission. (Source: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants)
source_refs: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants

options.text: Hepatitis B immunoglobulin 100 IU plus birth-dose monovalent vaccine within 12 hours of birth
options.rationale: Infants born to HBsAg positive mothers receive HBIG 100 IU plus birth-dose monovalent hepatitis B vaccine within 12 hours of birth, then complete the routine schedule, with post-vaccination serology at 9 to 12 months. (Source: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants)
source_refs: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants

options.text: Defer infant immunisation until maternal HBV DNA is repeated postpartum to confirm need
options.rationale: Maternal HBsAg positivity at booking is sufficient indication for neonatal HBIG and vaccine within 12 hours; deferring risks vertical transmission. (Source: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants)
source_refs: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section to reduce vertical hepatitis B transmission risk for the infant
options.rationale: Elective caesarean is not recommended to prevent vertical HBV transmission; appropriate neonatal immunoprophylaxis is the proven intervention. (Source: RANZCOG: Routine antenatal assessment)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Routine antenatal assessment

options.text: Test cord blood for HBsAg and only immunise the infant if cord blood comes back positive
options.rationale: Cord blood HBsAg testing is not used to direct prophylaxis; the high stakes of perinatal HBV mandate empiric HBIG and birth dose vaccine in all exposed infants. (Source: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants)
source_refs: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants

explanation.summary: Infants of HBsAg positive mothers are given hepatitis B immunoglobulin 100 IU and the birth-dose monovalent vaccine within 12 hours of birth, followed by the routine vaccine schedule and post-vaccination serology at 9 to 12 months. Caesarean section is not used to reduce vertical transmission. Cord blood testing does not guide prophylaxis.
key_points: HBIG plus birth-dose vaccine within 12 hours for infants of HBsAg+ mothers
Routine schedule completes the series with serology at 9-12 months
Caesarean section does not reduce vertical hepatitis B transmission
explanation.pearls: If the mother has HBV DNA above 200,000 IU/mL in the third trimester, antiviral therapy with tenofovir reduces vertical transmission further and is recommended per Australian liver society guidance.

sources.label: NCIRS: Hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin in pregnancy and infants

subtopic_detail: HELLP at late preterm requires delivery
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 34 weeks 2 days of gestation presents to the birth suite with 3 days of right upper quadrant and epigastric pain, nausea and one episode of vomiting. She has no headache or visual disturbance. CTG is reactive. She is not in labour. Cervix is unfavourable with a Bishop score of 3. There is no vaginal bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 99% in room air, temp 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Right upper quadrant tenderness, hyperreflexia without clonus, no peripheral oedema, no abdominal tenderness elsewhere
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102, platelets 78, ALT 220, AST 250, LDH 800, urate 0.46, urine PCR 0.5, creatinine 78; coagulation normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Antihypertensive control with oral nifedipine and expectant management to 37 weeks
options.rationale: Expectant management is not appropriate in HELLP at 34 weeks given maternal risks of seizure, hepatic rupture and DIC; delivery is indicated after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: SOMANZ: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy guideline

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis and observation only with daily bloods on the ward
options.rationale: Magnesium is given for seizure prophylaxis but observation alone is inadequate; HELLP at this gestation mandates delivery after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: SOMANZ: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy guideline

options.text: IV labetalol, IV magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis, and proceed to delivery after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: HELLP at or beyond 34 weeks is an indication for delivery after BP control with IV labetalol and IV magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis; mode of delivery is individualised. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy

options.text: Urgent plasma exchange for suspected thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura instead of delivery
options.rationale: TTP is suggested by MAHA with neurological symptoms and renal injury and very low ADAMTS13; HELLP fits the clinical picture and is treated by delivery. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: SOMANZ: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy guideline

options.text: IM betamethasone 12 mg and delay delivery 48 hours to allow full course for lung maturation
options.rationale: Delaying delivery for a full steroid course is inappropriate when maternal HELLP is established and worsening; a single dose may be given but delivery should not be delayed. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy

explanation.summary: HELLP syndrome at or beyond 34 weeks is managed by delivery after maternal stabilisation with antihypertensives and IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis. Expectant management risks hepatic rupture, DIC and eclampsia. Steroids may be given but should not delay delivery when maternal status is deteriorating.
key_points: HELLP at or beyond 34 weeks is an indication for delivery after stabilisation
IV labetalol controls severe hypertension; IV magnesium sulfate prevents eclampsia
Expectant management risks hepatic rupture and DIC in established HELLP
explanation.pearls: Platelet count below 50, marked transaminitis, deteriorating renal function or any neurological symptoms mandate urgent delivery regardless of gestational age in HELLP.

sources.label: SOMANZ: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy guideline

subtopic_detail: PPROM at 28 weeks expectant management bundle
stem: A 25-year-old G1 at 28 weeks 4 days of gestation presents 4 hours after a sudden gush of clear fluid per vagina, with continued small leakage. She has no contractions, abdominal pain or vaginal bleeding. She has no fevers or other systemic symptoms. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Group B streptococcus status is unknown.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 84, BP 118/72, RR 16, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Sterile speculum: clear amniotic fluid pooling in posterior fornix, no offensive odour, cervix appears closed; uterus soft and non-tender; CTG reactive with fetal heart rate 145
data_table.Investigations: WCC 11.5, CRP 8, urinalysis negative; high vaginal swab and low vaginal swab for GBS sent

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Immediate induction with vaginal prostaglandin gel given adequate fetal lung maturity at this gestation
options.rationale: At 28 weeks lung maturity is incomplete and immediate induction is not appropriate; expectant management to 34 weeks is standard unless infection or fetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Discharge home with twice weekly outpatient review and a course of antenatal corticosteroids only
options.rationale: PPROM before 34 weeks requires admission for monitoring of infection, fetal wellbeing and labour onset; outpatient management is not standard in Australia. (Source: RWH Melbourne: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes guideline)
source_refs: RWH Melbourne: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes guideline

options.text: Oral nifedipine tocolysis for 48 hours to allow steroid course completion then proceed to delivery
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not routinely indicated in PPROM without active labour and there is no need to plan delivery at 30 weeks in the absence of infection or fetal distress. (Source: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Admit, IM betamethasone, IV erythromycin, magnesium sulfate if delivery imminent, expectant care to 34 weeks
options.rationale: Standard PPROM care between 24 and 34 weeks includes admission, betamethasone, latency antibiotic with erythromycin for 10 days, magnesium sulfate neuroprotection if delivery imminent before 30 weeks, and expectant management to 34 weeks unless infection or fetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Emergency caesarean section to avoid chorioamnionitis given the risk of prolonged latency on the ward
options.rationale: Caesarean section is not indicated without infection, labour or fetal compromise; prematurity carries greater immediate risk than expectant management with monitoring. (Source: RWH Melbourne: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes guideline)
source_refs: RWH Melbourne: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes guideline

explanation.summary: PPROM between 24 and 34 weeks is managed with admission, antenatal corticosteroids, a 10-day latency antibiotic such as erythromycin, magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection if delivery is imminent before 30 weeks, and expectant management to 34 weeks. Delivery is hastened if there is chorioamnionitis, abruption or fetal compromise.
key_points: Expectant management of PPROM 24-34 weeks if no infection or fetal compromise
Latency antibiotics with erythromycin for 10 days reduce chorioamnionitis
Magnesium sulfate is given for neuroprotection if delivery is imminent before 30 weeks
explanation.pearls: Daily temperature, fetal heart rate auscultation, weekly CRP and twice weekly CTG with weekly biometry guide ongoing monitoring; delivery is advised by 34 weeks given the rising risk of chorioamnionitis.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes
RWH Melbourne: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes guideline

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 38 weeks of gestation presents in established labour. Her previous baby had culture-confirmed early-onset group B streptococcal sepsis at 36 hours of life and required NICU admission. She has no penicillin allergy. Her current pregnancy has been uncomplicated. On examination she is in active labour with intact membranes and a cervix dilated 5 cm.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.9 degrees Celsius, HR 88, BP 118/72, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Cephalic presentation, fetal heart rate 145 with reassuring CTG, uterine contractions 3 in 10 minutes lasting 60 seconds; cervix 5 cm dilated with intact membranes

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate intrapartum chemoprophylaxis?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg six-hourly during labour as outpatient style cover
options.rationale: Oral cefalexin does not provide adequate intrapartum tissue and fetal levels for GBS prophylaxis; IV therapy is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: IV vancomycin 1 g eight-hourly during labour due to GBS resistance concerns nationally
options.rationale: IV vancomycin is reserved for confirmed severe penicillin allergy and resistant isolates; it is not first-line in penicillin-tolerant women. (Source: RWH Melbourne: Group B streptococcus prophylaxis guideline)
source_refs: RWH Melbourne: Group B streptococcus prophylaxis guideline

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until membrane rupture has been present for more than 18 hours
options.rationale: Antibiotics are commenced at presentation in established labour when an indication is present, not deferred for membrane rupture duration. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g eight-hourly during labour as first-line prophylaxis
options.rationale: IV cefazolin is the option for non-severe penicillin allergy; in penicillin-tolerant women IV benzylpenicillin is first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g four-hourly until delivery completes
options.rationale: Previous infant with early-onset GBS sepsis is an indication for intrapartum chemoprophylaxis; IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g four-hourly until delivery is the Australian first-line regimen. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Intrapartum GBS chemoprophylaxis is indicated for previous infant with GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, positive screening swab, or unknown status with risk factors. The Australian first-line regimen is IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g four-hourly until delivery. Cefazolin is used for non-severe penicillin allergy; vancomycin for severe allergy or resistant strains.
key_points: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g q4h is first-line GBS prophylaxis
Previous GBS-affected infant is an automatic indication for prophylaxis
Cefazolin substitutes for non-severe allergy; vancomycin for severe allergy
explanation.pearls: Effective prophylaxis requires at least 4 hours of IV antibiotic before birth; consider early antibiotic if labour is progressing rapidly with a previous affected infant.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Maternal group B streptococcus in pregnancy
RWH Melbourne: Group B streptococcus prophylaxis guideline
